1: The superscription contains two titles: al-tašḥēt (Do Not Destroy — a tune name also used for Pss 58–59; cf. the vintage-song in Isaiah 65:8) and miktam of David when he fled from Saul in the cave — referring to either the cave of Adullam (1 Samuel 22:1) or En Gedi (1 Samuel 24:3). Both caves were places of refuge during Saul's pursuit. "Shadow of your wings" (bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā) is specifically temple imagery: the cherubim of the ark of the covenant spread their wings over the mercy seat (Exodus 25:20; Psalms 37:9), creating a "shadow" of divine protection over the most sacred space. To take shelter under YHWH's wings is to enter the sanctuary's protective space — wherever the speaker is, the temple's covering extends. The formula reappears in Psalms 17:8; Psalms 36:7; Psalms 61:4; Psalms 63:7; Psalms 91:4.
2: "I cry out to God Most High, to God who carries out his purpose for me" — ʾEl ʿelyôn (God Most High) is the ancient title used by Melchizedek of Salem (Genesis 14:18–22), who blessed Abraham as creator of heaven and earth. The title predates specifically Israelite theology and was a shared ANE designation for the supreme deity — used in Ugaritic texts for El as head of the divine council. Israel's appropriation of this title claimed that YHWH is that Most High God. "Who carries out his purpose for me" (gōmēr ʿālay) — gāmar means to bring to completion, to perform fully — the God who finishes what he starts for his covenant people.
3: "He will send from heaven and rescue me... God will send his steadfast love and his faithfulness" — hăsĕdô weʾămittô (his ḥesed and his faithfulness/truth). This pair is the Sinai self-revelation formula of Exodus 34:6 — "abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness" — now understood as two divine attributes sent from heaven on a rescue mission. In the Psalter's theology, divine rescue is not arbitrary power but an expression of the specific character God revealed at Sinai: covenant-loyal love and unfailing truthfulness. The formula reappears in Psalms 40:11; Psalms 57:10; Psalms 61:7; Psalms 85:10; Psalms 89:14; Psalms 115:1; Psalms 138:2.
4: "My soul is surrounded by lions; I must lie down among those who breathe fire — human beings whose teeth are spears and arrows and whose tongue is a sharp sword" — the enemies are described through overlapping animal and weapon metaphors. Lions (lĕbāʾîm, powerful lions) surround the speaker; their fiery breath (lahăṭîm, the ones who kindle/inflame — possibly an Aramaic loanword) may be a metaphor for harsh, scalding words. The teeth-as-weapons image (spears, arrows, swords) is shared with Psalms 55:21 and Joel 1:6 — the mouth weaponized. In both legal and military contexts, the tongue was understood as a lethal instrument in ANE culture (Proverbs 12:18; Psalms 25:18; Psalms 64:3).
5: "Be exalted above the heavens, O God; let your glory be over all the earth!" — the first occurrence of the liturgical refrain (repeated in Psalms 57:11) that frames the psalm. Rûm ʿal-hašāmayim ʾĕlōhîm ʿal kol-hāʾāreṣ kĕbôdekā. The prayer calls for divine exaltation above the entire created order — heavens and earth together. Kābôd (glory, weightiness, presence) is the visible manifestation of divine majesty that filled the tabernacle (Exodus 40:34–35) and temple (1 Kings 8:11) at their dedications. The refrain at the psalm's center and close creates a bracket of cosmic doxology around the lament — the crisis is placed within the larger frame of universal divine sovereignty.
6: "They spread a net for my feet; my soul was beaten down; they dug a pit in my path, but they themselves have fallen into it" — the trap-and-pit imagery describes deliberate ambush. The pivotal reversal in the second half of the verse employs what scholars call the "boomerang motif" — the trap laid for the innocent catches the trapper instead (Psalms 7:15–16; Psalms 9:15–16; Psalms 35:7–8; Proverbs 26:27; Ecclesiastes 10:8). This is not coincidence in the Psalter's theology but the structural logic of covenant justice: those who deploy wickedness against the righteous find that their weapons turn against themselves. The pattern is eschatological as well as historical — the final reversal is guaranteed even when not yet visible.
7: "My heart is fixed, O God, my heart is firm; I will sing and make melody!" — nākôn libbî (my heart is established, firm, resolved). The word nākôn (from kûn — to be established, set firmly) is a technical term for structural stability: a throne that is established, a kingdom that is founded, a person who is settled rather than wavering. The fixed heart of Psalms 57:7 contrasts with the "writhing heart" of Psalms 55:4 — the movement within this cluster of Psalms is from interior turmoil to interior stability. The decision to sing and make music (ʾāšîrāh waʾăzammĕrāh) follows from the fixed heart — resolved trust produces worship.
8: "Awake, my honor! Awake, harp and lyre! I will rouse the dawn!" — ʾāʿîrāh hašāḥar (I will rouse the dawn). The speaker addresses his own "honor" (kābôd — probably the soul or spirit; cf. Psalms 7:5; Psalms 16:9; Psalms 30:12; Psalms 108:1) to wake for praise. Then the musical instruments are addressed — a personification that animates the worship setting. The climactic statement, "I will rouse the dawn," reverses the expected relationship: normally the dawn wakes the sleeping human; here the praising human wakes before dawn, so eager for worship that he anticipates the sunrise. Dawn (šāḥar) was the appointed time of divine rescue in the Psalter (Psalms 46:5; Psalms 90:14; Lamentations 3:23) — the psalmist rises to meet it.
9: "I will give you thanks, O Lord, among the peoples; I will sing your praise among the nations" — the scope of praise is universal: ʿammîm (peoples, ethnic groups) and lĕʾummîm (nations). This verse is quoted by Paul in Romans 15:9 as evidence that the OT itself anticipated Gentile participation in Israel's praise of God — "as it is written: 'Therefore I will praise you among the Gentiles, and sing to your name.'" The universality of the psalm's praise aligns with the Psalter's vision (Psalms 22:27–31; Psalms 47:1–9; Psalms 96:3; Psalms 98:2–3) of all nations ultimately drawn into the worship of YHWH.
10: "For your steadfast love reaches to the heavens, your faithfulness to the skies" — ḥasdĕkā ʿad šāmāyim weʾămittĕkā ʿad šĕḥāqîm. The cosmic dimensions of divine attributes: ḥesed (steadfast love) stretches to the heavens; ʾĕmet (faithfulness/truth) reaches to the skies (šĕḥāqîm — the clouds or atmosphere). The vertical measurement is not merely hyperbolic — it asserts that the divine character is as foundational and encompassing as the heavens themselves. This verse is nearly identical to Psalms 36:5, where the same cosmic measurement describes ḥesed and ʾĕmûnāh alongside God's righteousness as deep as the great abyss.
11: The liturgical refrain of Psalms 57:5 repeated verbatim: "Be exalted above the heavens, O God; let your glory be over all the earth!" This closing return to the doxological frame encloses the entire psalm within a bracket of praise. The structure — refrain / lament and trust / refrain — models a movement that Second Temple liturgy would formalize: crisis held within the larger container of divine sovereignty and worship. Psalms with this envelope structure (also Ps 8, 46, 107, 118) demonstrate that the Psalter's editors shaped individual psalms into theological arguments through structure as much as through content.
